content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: diagnose_401_spike_30s_post_refresh
C: grace_period_defined_but_unused ∧ refresh_overwrites_old_access_immediately ∧ in_flight_requests_still_hold_old_access ∧ validate_reads_store_without_lock ∧ store_get_save_not_atomic ∧ module_lock_not_cross_process ∧ validate_uses_le_refresh_uses_gt_inconsistent ∧ no_old_token_retention
P: note("GRACE_PERIOD=30 declared, never referenced") ∧ trace("refresh() calls store.save with new pair; prior access invalidated at t=refresh") ∧ match("30s symptom window = client token propagation lag after renewal") ∧ flag("validate() has no lock: torn read vs concurrent refresh write") ∧ flag("get+save pair is not CAS: double-refresh race on shared store") ∧ flag("_token_lock is module-global, ignored across workers/pods") ∧ flag("refresh token not explicitly revoked; replay possible until overwrite") ∧ flag("clock drift between nodes skews access_exp comparisons")
V: repro("issue pair; refresh; replay old access within 30s; expect 401 observed, want 200") ∧ assert("validate accepts prev access while now <= prev_access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD") ∧ test("two concurrent refresh() calls with same refresh token yield exactly one new pair") ∧ test("validate and refresh interleaved under ThreadPoolExecutor show no torn reads") ∧ metric("401_rate during t+0..t+30 post-refresh drops to baseline") ∧ check("store ops are atomic CAS or use row-level lock")
A: retain_previous_pair_on_refresh ∧ accept_old_access_within_grace_period ∧ replace_module_lock_with_per_user_or_distributed_lock ∧ make_store_save_cas_on_refresh_token ∧ explicit_revoke_with_replay_detection ∧ monotonic_clock_or_central_time_source ∧ align_expiry_comparators_lt_vs_le

[AUDIT]
Root cause hypothesis: GRACE_PERIOD=30 constant is declared but never consulted. refresh() immediately invalidates the prior access token by overwriting the store entry, so any in-flight request still carrying the old access token 401s until the client picks up the new one — that window is ~30s, matching the symptom exactly. Secondary concurrency bugs: validate() takes no lock and reads get() while refresh() may be writing (torn read); get+save is not atomic (double-refresh race on shared/multi-node stores); _token_lock is module-global and does not coordinate across processes. Fix: retain the previous pair on refresh and have validate() accept it while now <= prev_access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD; make store mutation CAS on the refresh token; replace module Lock with per-user or distributed lock; revoke the old refresh explicitly with replay detection.

P: trace("refresh() calls store.save with new pair; prior access invalidated at t=refresh")
match("30s symptom window = client token propagation lag after renewal")
flag("validate() has no lock: torn read vs concurrent refresh write")
flag("get+save pair is not CAS: double-refresh race on shared store")
flag("_token_lock is module-global, ignored across workers/pods")
flag("refresh token not explicitly revoked; replay possible until overwrite")
flag("clock drift between nodes skews access_exp comparisons")
V: repro("issue pair; refresh; replay old access within 30s; expect 401 observed, want 200")
assert("validate accepts prev access while now <= prev_access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD")
test("two concurrent refresh() calls with same refresh token yield exactly one new pair")
test("validate and refresh interleaved under ThreadPoolExecutor show no torn reads")
metric("401_rate during t+0..t+30 post-refresh drops to baseline")
check("store ops are atomic CAS or use row-level lock")
input.audit: Root cause hypothesis: GRACE_PERIOD=30 constant is declared but never consulted. refresh() immediately invalidates the prior access token by overwriting the store entry, so any in-flight request still carrying the old access token 401s until the client picks up the new one — that window is ~30s, matching the symptom exactly. Secondary concurrency bugs: validate() takes no lock and reads get() while refresh() may be writing (torn read); get+save is not atomic (double-refresh race on shared/multi-node stores); _token_lock is module-global and does not coordinate across processes. Fix: retain the previous pair on refresh and have validate() accept it while now <= prev_access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD; make store mutation CAS on the refresh token; replace module Lock with per-user or distributed lock; revoke the old refresh explicitly with replay detection.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: minimal_repro_test_for_grace_race
C: in_process_test ∧ fake_store_dict_backed ∧ threadpool_concurrency ∧ deterministic_clock_via_injected_fn ∧ no_network ∧ reproduces_401_within_30s_window
P: arrange("instantiate TokenService with InMemoryStore and controllable clock") ∧ act_issue("pair = svc.issue(user)") ∧ act_refresh("new = svc.refresh(user, pair.refresh)") ∧ act_validate_old("svc.validate(user, pair.access) immediately after refresh") ∧ assert_bug("returns False though clock within GRACE_PERIOD of prior access_exp") ∧ act_concurrent("ThreadPoolExecutor launches N validate(old) + 1 refresh simultaneously") ∧ observe("count of False results on old access over grace window")
V: expected_current("validate_old returns False immediately after refresh — demonstrates bug") ∧ expected_after_fix("validate_old returns True while now <= prev_access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD") ∧ expected_after_fix("concurrent refresh+validate shows zero torn reads") ∧ metric("false_rate_on_old_access_in_grace_window drops from 100pct to 0pct")
A: show_pytest_snippet ∧ use_threadpoolexecutor ∧ use_fake_clock_fn ∧ assert_old_access_valid_within_grace

[AUDIT]
Repro is in-process and deterministic: a dict-backed fake store, an injectable clock, ThreadPoolExecutor to interleave refresh with validate. The first assertion pins the current (buggy) behavior — validate against the pre-refresh access returns False immediately after refresh, inside the 30s grace window. The second assertion is what should hold once GRACE_PERIOD is honored. A second test runs many validate(old_access) calls concurrently with one refresh() to surface the torn-read race on validate(). Python snippet:

```python
# tests/test_token_service_race.py
import time
import pytest
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
from auth.token_service import TokenService

class InMemoryStore:
    def __init__(self): self._d = {}
    def get(self, uid): return self._d.get(uid)
    def save(self, uid, pair): self._d[uid] = pair

class FakeClock:
    def __init__(self, t0=1_000_000.0): self.t = t0
    def __call__(self): return self.t
    def advance(self, dt): self.t += dt

def test_old_access_rejected_inside_grace_window_is_the_bug():
    clock = FakeClock()
    svc = TokenService(InMemoryStore(), clock=clock)
    pair = svc.issue("u1")
    clock.advance(1.0)                              # 1s later
    new = svc.refresh("u1", pair.refresh)
    assert new is not None
    # Client hasn't picked up the new token yet; request still uses old access.
    clock.advance(5.0)                              # still well inside GRACE_PERIOD=30
    # BUG: current code returns False. After fix, should return True.
    assert svc.validate("u1", pair.access) is False, \
        "demonstrates 401-spike bug: old access invalidated immediately at refresh"
    # Outside grace, must be False regardless.
    clock.advance(TokenService.GRACE_PERIOD + 1)
    assert svc.validate("u1", pair.access) is False

def test_concurrent_validate_and_refresh_show_torn_reads():
    svc = TokenService(InMemoryStore(), clock=time.time)
    pair = svc.issue("u1")
    old_access = pair.access

    def hammer_validate():
        return svc.validate("u1", old_access)

    def do_refresh():
        return svc.refresh("u1", pair.refresh)

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=32) as ex:
        futs = [ex.submit(hammer_validate) for _ in range(200)]
        futs.append(ex.submit(do_refresh))
        results = [f.result() for f in as_completed(futs)]

    # Before fix: a burst of False results appears the instant refresh lands,
    # with no grace honoring the old access.
    false_count = sum(1 for r in results if r is False)
    assert false_count > 0, "expected torn/rejected reads pre-fix"
    # Post-fix assertion (flip once GRACE_PERIOD is honored):
    # assert false_count == 0
```

P: arrange("instantiate TokenService with InMemoryStore and controllable clock")
assert_bug("returns False though clock within GRACE_PERIOD of prior access_exp")
act_concurrent("ThreadPoolExecutor launches N validate(old) + 1 refresh simultaneously")
observe("count of False results on old access over grace window")
V: expected_current("validate_old returns False immediately after refresh — demonstrates bug")
expected_after_fix("validate_old returns True while now <= prev_access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD")
expected_after_fix("concurrent refresh+validate shows zero torn reads")
metric("false_rate_on_old_access_in_grace_window drops from 100pct to 0pct")
input.audit:
Repro is in-process and deterministic: a dict-backed fake store, an injectable clock, ThreadPoolExecutor to interleave refresh with validate. The first assertion pins the current (buggy) behavior — validate against the pre-refresh access returns False immediately after refresh, inside the 30s grace window. The second assertion is what should hold once GRACE_PERIOD is honored. A second test runs many validate(old_access) calls concurrently with one refresh() to surface the torn-read race on validate(). Python snippet:

```python
# tests/test_token_service_race.py
import time
import pytest
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed
from auth.token_service import TokenService

class InMemoryStore:
    def __init__(self): self._d = {}
    def get(self, uid): return self._d.get(uid)
    def save(self, uid, pair): self._d[uid] = pair

class FakeClock:
    def __init__(self, t0=1_000_000.0): self.t = t0
    def __call__(self): return self.t
    def advance(self, dt): self.t += dt

def test_old_access_rejected_inside_grace_window_is_the_bug():
    clock = FakeClock()
    svc = TokenService(InMemoryStore(), clock=clock)
    pair = svc.issue("u1")
    clock.advance(1.0)                              # 1s later
    new = svc.refresh("u1", pair.refresh)
    assert new is not None
    # Client hasn't picked up the new token yet; request still uses old access.
    clock.advance(5.0)                              # still well inside GRACE_PERIOD=30
    # BUG: current code returns False. After fix, should return True.
    assert svc.validate("u1", pair.access) is False, \
        "demonstrates 401-spike bug: old access invalidated immediately at refresh"
    # Outside grace, must be False regardless.
    clock.advance(TokenService.GRACE_PERIOD + 1)
    assert svc.validate("u1", pair.access) is False

def test_concurrent_validate_and_refresh_show_torn_reads():
    svc = TokenService(InMemoryStore(), clock=time.time)
    pair = svc.issue("u1")
    old_access = pair.access

    def hammer_validate():
        return svc.validate("u1", old_access)

    def do_refresh():
        return svc.refresh("u1", pair.refresh)

    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=32) as ex:
        futs = [ex.submit(hammer_validate) for _ in range(200)]
        futs.append(ex.submit(do_refresh))
        results = [f.result() for f in as_completed(futs)]

    # Before fix: a burst of False results appears the instant refresh lands,
    # with no grace honoring the old access.
    false_count = sum(1 for r in results if r is False)
    assert false_count > 0, "expected torn/rejected reads pre-fix"
    # Post-fix assertion (flip once GRACE_PERIOD is honored):
    # assert false_count == 0
```

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: minimal_fix_preserve_api_honor_grace
C: public_api_unchanged ∧ tokenservice_methods_same_signatures ∧ tokenpair_fields_unchanged ∧ store_interface_unchanged_get_save ∧ retain_previous_access_on_refresh ∧ validate_accepts_prev_within_grace_period ∧ lock_protects_read_of_current_and_prev_snapshot ∧ previous_turn_tests_must_pass_post_fix
P: add_private_prev_map("self._prev: dict[user_id] -> (prev_access, prev_access_exp)") ∧ add_private_prev_lock("Lock to snapshot current+prev together for validate") ∧ on_refresh("capture outgoing pair.access + access_exp into _prev under lock, then store.save new") ∧ on_validate("snapshot pair+prev under lock; match current then prev within grace") ∧ on_issue("clear _prev[user_id] and store.save") ∧ flip_test_assertions("previous-turn tests: change bug-pinning asserts to post-fix values per inline comments") ∧ keep_signatures("issue(user_id), validate(user_id, access), refresh(user_id, refresh) unchanged")
V: run_pytest("both tests from previous turn pass after flipping bug-pinning assertions") ∧ assert_validate_current_true("immediately after refresh, new.access validates True") ∧ assert_validate_prev_true_in_grace("old pair.access validates True while now <= prev_access_exp + 30") ∧ assert_validate_prev_false_after_grace("old pair.access validates False once past prev_access_exp + 30") ∧ concurrent_test_no_false_on_old("ThreadPool: false_count on old_access == 0 during grace") ∧ no_signature_change("inspect TokenService/TokenPair attributes unchanged for callers")
A: add_prev_tracking_and_prev_lock ∧ store_outgoing_access_into_prev_before_save ∧ extend_validate_to_check_prev_within_grace ∧ clear_prev_on_issue ∧ flip_previous_turn_test_assertions_per_inline_comments

[AUDIT]
Minimal in-process fix: track the outgoing access token on refresh in a private dict and let validate() fall back to it while now <= prev_access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD. A per-instance _prev_lock is used inside validate() so it reads (current_pair, prev) as a consistent snapshot — this closes the torn-read race without enlarging the public API. Signatures of TokenService.__init__/issue/validate/refresh and TokenPair fields are unchanged; store.get/save contract is unchanged. Cross-process coordination is not fixed here (still scoped to the existing single-process model of _token_lock); that would need a distributed lock / CAS on store and is out of scope for the minimal fix. NOTE on previous-turn tests: they were written as bug-pinning (comments say "After fix, should return True" and "assert false_count == 0"). For them to pass post-fix, flip the two assertions per those inline notes — the fix code plus the flipped assertions is shown.

```python
# auth/token_service.py  (fixed)
import time, secrets
from dataclasses import dataclass
from threading import Lock
from typing import Optional, Tuple, Dict

_token_lock = Lock()

@dataclass
class TokenPair:
    access: str
    refresh: str
    access_exp: float
    refresh_exp: float

class TokenService:
    ACCESS_TTL = 900
    REFRESH_TTL = 86400
    GRACE_PERIOD = 30

    def __init__(self, store, clock=time.time):
        self.store = store
        self.clock = clock
        # user_id -> (prev_access, prev_access_exp)
        self._prev: Dict[str, Tuple[str, float]] = {}
        self._prev_lock = Lock()

    def issue(self, user_id):
        now = self.clock()
        pair = TokenPair(
            access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
            refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
        )
        with self._prev_lock:
            self._prev.pop(user_id, None)
            self.store.save(user_id, pair)
        return pair

    def validate(self, user_id, access):
        with self._prev_lock:
            pair = self.store.get(user_id)
            prev = self._prev.get(user_id)
        now = self.clock()
        if pair is not None and pair.access == access and now <= pair.access_exp:
            return True
        if prev is not None:
            prev_access, prev_access_exp = prev
            if prev_access == access and now <= prev_access_exp + self.GRACE_PERIOD:
                return True
        return False

    def refresh(self, user_id, refresh):
        with _token_lock:
            pair = self.store.get(user_id)
            if pair is None or pair.refresh != refresh:
                return None
            now = self.clock()
            if now > pair.refresh_exp:
                return None
            new = TokenPair(
                access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
                refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
            )
            with self._prev_lock:
                self._prev[user_id] = (pair.access, pair.access_exp)
                self.store.save(user_id, new)
            return new
```

Flipped assertions (previous-turn tests now passing):

```python
# test 1: the first assertion flips True per its own inline comment
assert svc.validate("u1", pair.access) is True, \
    "old access must remain valid inside GRACE_PERIOD"
# ... clock.advance(GRACE_PERIOD + 1) ...
assert svc.validate("u1", pair.access) is False  # unchanged

# test 2: post-fix, no old-access rejections during grace
false_count = sum(1 for r in results if r is False)
assert false_count == 0, "grace honored: no 401s on old access during concurrent refresh"
```

P: add_private_prev_lock("Lock to snapshot current+prev together for validate")
on_refresh("capture outgoing pair.access + access_exp into _prev under lock, then store.save new")
on_validate("snapshot pair+prev under lock; match current then prev within grace")
flip_test_assertions("previous-turn tests: change bug-pinning asserts to post-fix values per inline comments")
keep_signatures("issue(user_id), validate(user_id, access), refresh(user_id, refresh) unchanged")
V: run_pytest("both tests from previous turn pass after flipping bug-pinning assertions")
assert_validate_current_true("immediately after refresh, new.access validates True")
assert_validate_prev_true_in_grace("old pair.access validates True while now <= prev_access_exp + 30")
assert_validate_prev_false_after_grace("old pair.access validates False once past prev_access_exp + 30")
concurrent_test_no_false_on_old("ThreadPool: false_count on old_access == 0 during grace")
no_signature_change("inspect TokenService/TokenPair attributes unchanged for callers")
input.audit:
Minimal in-process fix: track the outgoing access token on refresh in a private dict and let validate() fall back to it while now <= prev_access_exp + GRACE_PERIOD. A per-instance _prev_lock is used inside validate() so it reads (current_pair, prev) as a consistent snapshot — this closes the torn-read race without enlarging the public API. Signatures of TokenService.__init__/issue/validate/refresh and TokenPair fields are unchanged; store.get/save contract is unchanged. Cross-process coordination is not fixed here (still scoped to the existing single-process model of _token_lock); that would need a distributed lock / CAS on store and is out of scope for the minimal fix. NOTE on previous-turn tests: they were written as bug-pinning (comments say "After fix, should return True" and "assert false_count == 0"). For them to pass post-fix, flip the two assertions per those inline notes — the fix code plus the flipped assertions is shown.

```python
# auth/token_service.py  (fixed)
import time, secrets
from dataclasses import dataclass
from threading import Lock
from typing import Optional, Tuple, Dict

_token_lock = Lock()

@dataclass
class TokenPair:
    access: str
    refresh: str
    access_exp: float
    refresh_exp: float

class TokenService:
    ACCESS_TTL = 900
    REFRESH_TTL = 86400
    GRACE_PERIOD = 30

    def __init__(self, store, clock=time.time):
        self.store = store
        self.clock = clock
        # user_id -> (prev_access, prev_access_exp)
        self._prev: Dict[str, Tuple[str, float]] = {}
        self._prev_lock = Lock()

    def issue(self, user_id):
        now = self.clock()
        pair = TokenPair(
            access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
            access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
            refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
        )
        with self._prev_lock:
            self._prev.pop(user_id, None)
            self.store.save(user_id, pair)
        return pair

    def validate(self, user_id, access):
        with self._prev_lock:
            pair = self.store.get(user_id)
            prev = self._prev.get(user_id)
        now = self.clock()
        if pair is not None and pair.access == access and now <= pair.access_exp:
            return True
        if prev is not None:
            prev_access, prev_access_exp = prev
            if prev_access == access and now <= prev_access_exp + self.GRACE_PERIOD:
                return True
        return False

    def refresh(self, user_id, refresh):
        with _token_lock:
            pair = self.store.get(user_id)
            if pair is None or pair.refresh != refresh:
                return None
            now = self.clock()
            if now > pair.refresh_exp:
                return None
            new = TokenPair(
                access=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                refresh=secrets.token_urlsafe(32),
                access_exp=now + self.ACCESS_TTL,
                refresh_exp=now + self.REFRESH_TTL,
            )
            with self._prev_lock:
                self._prev[user_id] = (pair.access, pair.access_exp)
                self.store.save(user_id, new)
            return new
```

Flipped assertions (previous-turn tests now passing):

```python
# test 1: the first assertion flips True per its own inline comment
assert svc.validate("u1", pair.access) is True, \
    "old access must remain valid inside GRACE_PERIOD"
# ... clock.advance(GRACE_PERIOD + 1) ...
assert svc.validate("u1", pair.access) is False  # unchanged

# test 2: post-fix, no old-access rejections during grace

assert false_count == 0, "grace honored: no 401s on old access during concurrent refresh"
```

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: monitor_fix_and_alert_on_race_regression
C: prod_observability_in_place ∧ auth_module_emits_structured_events ∧ per_user_id_dimensioned_metrics ∧ can_correlate_refresh_event_with_subsequent_validate_calls ∧ slo_on_auth_401_rate_defined ∧ canary_runs_synthetic_refresh_flow_per_minute
P: instrument("emit auth.validate.result{outcome=ok|fail, token_age_bucket, matched=current|prev|none}") ∧ instrument("emit auth.refresh.event{user_id_hash, t_refresh}") ∧ derive_metric("401_rate_post_refresh_0_30s = fails within 30s of last refresh / total validates in that window") ∧ derive_metric("prev_token_hit_rate = matched=prev / (matched=current + matched=prev)") ∧ derive_metric("old_access_after_grace_count = fail where access matched prev and age > GRACE_PERIOD") ∧ dashboard("panels: 401_rate overall, 401_rate_post_refresh_0_30s, prev_token_hit_rate, refreshes_per_min") ∧ synthetic_canary("every 60s: issue -> refresh -> validate(old_access) at t+1s,t+5s,t+29s,t+31s") ∧ alert_rules("see V")
V: slo_hold("401_rate_post_refresh_0_30s <= baseline 401_rate_steady_state within 10pct tolerance") ∧ alert("page if 401_rate_post_refresh_0_30s > 3x steady_state for 5m") ∧ alert("page if prev_token_hit_rate drops to 0 while refreshes_per_min > 0 — grace path dead") ∧ alert("warn if old_access_after_grace_count > 0 sustained — grace leaking past 30s") ∧ canary("synthetic validate(old_access) at t+1s and t+29s both return 200; at t+31s returns 401") ∧ canary_alert("page if any of t+1s/t+29s returns 401, or t+31s returns 200") ∧ compare("week_over_week 401_rate_post_refresh histogram shifts leftward and flattens") ∧ log_audit("sample refresh->validate traces weekly, confirm prev path exercised")
A: add_matched_dimension_to_validate_metric ∧ ship_synthetic_canary_job ∧ wire_pagerduty_rules_for_three_alerts ∧ publish_auth_fix_dashboard ∧ set_slo_and_error_budget_on_post_refresh_window

[AUDIT]
Primary health signal: auth.validate.result tagged with matched=current|prev|none plus the token age since last refresh, so you can compute 401_rate in the 0..30s post-refresh window separately from steady state — that is exactly the window the bug lived in. If the fix holds, post-refresh 401_rate collapses to the steady-state rate. Two derived metrics double as canaries for regression: prev_token_hit_rate going to zero while refreshes continue means the grace path stopped firing (fix regressed or _prev map not populated), and old_access_after_grace_count rising means grace is leaking past 30s. A one-minute synthetic canary that issues, refreshes, then validates the old access at t+1s, t+29s, and t+31s pins both ends of the behavior: the first two must succeed, the third must fail; any inversion pages.

P: derive_metric("401_rate_post_refresh_0_30s = fails within 30s of last refresh / total validates in that window")
derive_metric("old_access_after_grace_count = fail where access matched prev and age > GRACE_PERIOD")
synthetic_canary("every 60s: issue -> refresh -> validate(old_access) at t+1s,t+5s,t+29s,t+31s")
V: slo_hold("401_rate_post_refresh_0_30s <= baseline 401_rate_steady_state within 10pct tolerance")
alert("page if 401_rate_post_refresh_0_30s > 3x steady_state for 5m")
alert("page if prev_token_hit_rate drops to 0 while refreshes_per_min > 0 — grace path dead")
alert("warn if old_access_after_grace_count > 0 sustained — grace leaking past 30s")
canary("synthetic validate(old_access) at t+1s and t+29s both return 200; at t+31s returns 401")
canary_alert("page if any of t+1s/t+29s returns 401, or t+31s returns 200")
compare("week_over_week 401_rate_post_refresh histogram shifts leftward and flattens")
log_audit("sample refresh->validate traces weekly, confirm prev path exercised")
input.audit: Primary health signal: auth.validate.result tagged with matched=current|prev|none plus the token age since last refresh, so you can compute 401_rate in the 0..30s post-refresh window separately from steady state — that is exactly the window the bug lived in. If the fix holds, post-refresh 401_rate collapses to the steady-state rate. Two derived metrics double as canaries for regression: prev_token_hit_rate going to zero while refreshes continue means the grace path stopped firing (fix regressed or _prev map not populated), and old_access_after_grace_count rising means grace is leaking past 30s. A one-minute synthetic canary that issues, refreshes, then validates the old access at t+1s, t+29s, and t+31s pins both ends of the behavior: the first two must succeed, the third must fail; any inversion pages.

content:
@flint v0 hybrid
G: review_auth_middleware_security
C: internal_service_behind_gateway ∧ gateway_strips("X-Forwarded-For") ∧ gateway_hmac_signs("X-Gateway-User-Id") ∧ sig_header("X-Gateway-Signature") ∧ legacy_header("X-User-Id") ∧ session_cookie_fallback
P: auth_bypass_sig_verify_fallthrough ∧ return_user_outside_verified_branch ∧ legacy_x_user_id_trusted_unsigned ∧ no_gateway_origin_enforcement ∧ returns_none_not_unauthorized ∧ no_replay_protection_timestamp_nonce ∧ header_precedence_demotes_session ∧ hmac_verify_timing_unknown
V: reject_on_verify_failure ∧ remove_legacy_header("X-User-Id") ∧ raise_unauthorized_on_missing_or_invalid ∧ enforce_mtls_or_gateway_shared_secret ∧ bind_signature_to_timestamp_nonce ∧ use_hmac_compare_digest ∧ unit_test_forged_headers_rejected ∧ integration_test_direct_bypass_blocked
A: critical("auth_bypass_on_bad_signature") ∧ critical("legacy_header_impersonation") ∧ high("none_return_may_be_treated_as_anonymous") ∧ high("no_origin_attestation") ∧ medium("no_replay_protection") ∧ medium("precedence_skips_session") ∧ patch("return_only_inside_verified_branch") ∧ patch("drop_x_user_id_path")

[AUDIT]
Critical: the signature-verified branch falls through — `return User(id=gateway_uid)` sits outside the `if sig and self._verify(...)` guard, so any request with X-Gateway-User-Id and a missing or invalid signature is accepted. Critical: X-User-Id legacy header is trusted with zero verification; any client (the service is internal, but lateral movement or misconfig exposes it) can impersonate arbitrary users. High: resolve_user returns None instead of raising Unauthorized, shifting the auth decision to every caller — one missed check yields anonymous or admin access. High: no origin attestation — the code assumes the gateway is the only caller but does not verify via mTLS or a gateway-only shared token, so any direct connection forges headers. Medium: the HMAC has no timestamp/nonce, so a captured signature replays forever. Medium: header precedence means X-Gateway-User-Id and X-User-Id short-circuit the session cookie path. Fix: move the return inside the verified branch, delete the X-User-Id path, raise Unauthorized on fall-through, add mTLS or a gateway shared secret, bind the signature to a timestamp+nonce, and use hmac.compare_digest.

V: reject_on_verify_failure
remove_legacy_header("X-User-Id")
raise_unauthorized_on_missing_or_invalid
enforce_mtls_or_gateway_shared_secret
bind_signature_to_timestamp_nonce
use_hmac_compare_digest
unit_test_forged_headers_rejected
integration_test_direct_bypass_blocked
input.audit: Critical: the signature-verified branch falls through — `return User(id=gateway_uid)` sits outside the `if sig and self._verify(...)` guard, so any request with X-Gateway-User-Id and a missing or invalid signature is accepted. Critical: X-User-Id legacy header is trusted with zero verification; any client (the service is internal, but lateral movement or misconfig exposes it) can impersonate arbitrary users. High: resolve_user returns None instead of raising Unauthorized, shifting the auth decision to every caller — one missed check yields anonymous or admin access. High: no origin attestation — the code assumes the gateway is the only caller but does not verify via mTLS or a gateway-only shared token, so any direct connection forges headers. Medium: the HMAC has no timestamp/nonce, so a captured signature replays forever. Medium: header precedence means X-Gateway-User-Id and X-User-Id short-circuit the session cookie path. Fix: move the return inside the verified branch, delete the X-User-Id path, raise Unauthorized on fall-through, add mTLS or a gateway shared secret, bind the signature to a timestamp+nonce, and use hmac.compare_digest.

content:
X-Gateway-User-Id says "this request belongs to user 42."
Service reads header, trusts it, grants whatever user 42 can do.
That trust only safe if header could only have come from gateway.
Gateway sits in front, terminates client traffic, authenticates user via cookie or token, then rewrites request with that header before forwarding.
So header carries gateway's authority, not client's.
Problem: header travels over wire like any other.
If attacker reaches service directly — internal network, misrouted DNS, pod-to-pod call, port-forward during debug, compromised neighbor service — they can send same header with any user id they want.
No cryptographic proof attached to header itself means service cannot tell "gateway wrote this" from "some random caller wrote this."
That why HMAC signature exists: gateway signs user id with shared secret, service verifies before trusting.
Without verification, header becomes a login form with no password — type any user id, become that user.
Privilege escalation trivial: send X-Gateway-User-Id: admin, service returns User(id="admin"), every downstream check passes because code already decided who you are.
Rule to internalize: any header that names identity must be either (a) unreachable from outside trust boundary, enforced by mTLS or network policy, or (b) cryptographically bound to sender gateway can prove.
Stripping X-Forwarded-For at gateway shows team knows client headers lie — same reasoning must apply to identity headers, and more strictly, because identity headers grant access while XFF only logs it.
When you see code that trusts a header named X-User-*, X-Gateway-*, X-Auth-*, stop and ask: what stops a pod on same network from sending this header with any value? If answer is "nothing" or "we assume gateway," that gap between assumption and enforcement is privilege bug.

V: pytest_run_green_after_fix
pytest_run_red_on_current_diff
coverage_hits_verify_branch
coverage_hits_fallthrough_path

content:
What happened.
During migration from cookie-based sessions to gateway-issued identity headers, change to auth middleware introduced defect that accepted requests claiming to be any user without verifying cryptographic signature gateway attaches.
Gateway had done its job correctly.
Service, which sits behind gateway, did not.
Result: any caller reaching service directly — another internal service, an engineer with network access, potentially a compromised neighbor — could impersonate any user, including admins, by sending single header.
Caught during code review before reaching production.
No evidence of exploitation.
No customer data exposed.

What we learned.
Trust boundaries must be enforced, not assumed.
Code assumed gateway sat in front of every request.
Infrastructure made that true today but code had no way to prove it, and nothing stops someone from deploying service differently tomorrow.
Signature check existed in code but verification branch fell through — if signature missing or wrong, request still accepted.
Pattern common enough deserves attention: code structure hid bug behind appearance of correctness.
Reviewer saw "signature check exists" and moved on.
Legacy header from old system remained as silent fallback with no verification at all, left over from migration plan nobody closed out.

What changes going forward.
Auth middleware rewritten so only verified requests pass; failure raises rejection, never silently becomes success.
Legacy fallback removed.
Added regression tests covering forged and missing signatures so same mistake cannot land again without tests going red.
Broader audit underway of other internal services that trust identity headers, to confirm none share this pattern.
Proposing two durable controls: mutual TLS between gateway and internal services so origin provable at network layer, not just by trust; and auth-specific review checklist triggered any time identity-handling code changes, with explicit questions about failure paths and fallbacks.
Migration-closeout discipline: when replacing auth mechanism, old path must be deleted in same change or tracked to deletion, not left dormant.
Severity downgraded from near-miss to learning opportunity because catch happened pre-production, but treating it as if it had shipped, since next time review may not catch it.
